text.p: Greetings and Happy New Year to you. I tried to stay within your radar in 2008 so you sort of have an idea what I was up to during the year. This is a new year and I hope to do a better job. I believe that I am living an exciting life. It’s hard to believe that serving the living God can be so exciting but it is. Not the travels, but what is accomplished. The travels can be hectic and tiring, sometimes the schedules even seem impossible. Towards the end of 2008, and I drove to Nigeria at the end of October, stayed for about a month and drove back to . A month later we were on the same road across the six border check points to – – and back in the reverse direction about three weeks later. We arrived in and I had only one day before I boarded Airways to be here in . It sounds crazy but it is satisfying to see what the Lord is doing.

So what is the Lord doing? The October/November trip had some ‘rest’ in built into it but the highlight was the evaluation in , of the amazing things God is doing in state of Nigeria in church planting among completely unreached people through a partnership project called end to end. People who had no idea who Jesus was are now giving their lives to Him and becoming part of new churches and the churches are also planting other churches. I am glad to have a part in that. The December/January trip was to spend Christmas at home but also to be at the all West Africa staff conference of Great Commission Movement. You should have been there at that conference. It was the best I have ever attended in my 25 years of involvement with this ministry. First we had the founding father of Great Commission Movement of , Rev.
there from the beginning to the end and he was a great inspiration to all of us. Then we had the first set of staff, Professor
and his wife, and the Director that took over from rev. , Professor
and his wife. It was such a great time. The inspiration from our new Director of Affairs is so timely. obviously knows where he wants to lead us, he is able to articulate it clearly so everyone should be able to understand it, and he seems to be able to find ways of persuading us all to be eager to follow him there. I believe that defines leadership. It was that said if you think you are leading but no one is following you, you are only going for a walk. is not going for a walk. We thank God for this young leader.

Well, I am writing you from boarding gate 14 of the
International Airport in . We should start boarding in a short while. But I reflect on this one week in and I thank God that he has directed me to spend my life serving Him full time. You know we are working on a follow up video series for the Jesus Film. There are six village episodes and at least the same number for the city. Representative staff from Western, Eastern and Southern and Francophone Africa, men and women have been here in with our script writer, to get the scripts for the village episodes ready for shooting. is probably the best scriptwriter of Christian films in Africa with more than 40 films that he himself has produced. is such a humble person. We all wanted to end up with the best product that is action packed but also true to and teaches all the principles needed for a new believer to grow in their faith. So with all our backgrounds from the to , from the to Nigeria, from the to , we dissected every word that has in these scripts. We grilled him to find out what he had in mind every point of the scripts made requests and suggestions that seemed impossible and asked to go and do it. Every time, he came back in the morning to surprise us. By the time we read the new script, all the problems are solved and he has so very creatively rewrote the script that everything we want is there. Amazing! Last night, and I hugged each other and said good bye for a while. He is now on his way back to , Nigeria, where his ministry office is. We meet again March and April this year to shoot the films here in . What a week this has been!

Please pray for as he goes back to his ministry, the Film Ministry, and for this product, that God will take care of all the details of the shooting and the strategy for use when it is ready. I look forward to it. When I get back to this afternoon, will meet me at the airport but we only have a few hour together before she heads out to for a week. Thanks for lifting us up.

State of Nigeria may not represent the ends of the earth to you, but to some it might as well be. Today is Saturday, April 18th. We ate breakfast at 7.30 and at 8:00, we packed ourselves into a two cabin Toyota truck, , and at the back, I was in the front seat and was driving. The three ladies are from . is a veteran with the Jesus film in West Africa and has been through many adventures in Nigeria before. is also not a stranger to Africa. She was with us in in May last year. But is a first timer. She just took the job replacing . ( is getting married in today). who drove is a veteran Jesus Film worker and discipler. He discipled the supervisors we will see today. We set out about 8:00 from Hotel where we have been staying, and we got on the - road, which is not exactly a super highway. I thought this was what the church planters meant in their song when they sing ba ndamu da gargada hayan ba (no matter the roughness of the road) until we got off the real road. Using the truck is the only way we could have accessed the villages we went today. We went on dirt roads or off road, and many times all we would see was a foot path used by pedestrians and motorcycles. If drove at 30 kilometers per hour, he was speeding. We went in and out of gullies that were flowing rivers during the raining season. I am glad it is still the dry season. , who is the supervisor of the church planters we are visiting today, sat in the back of the truck from until we were going of the road.

got down to pick up his motor cycle, which was brought to meet him there at the junction by , one of the church planters. He went to get black market fuel for his motorcycle while we moved on. It was not long before and overtook us. Motorcycles are faster than vehicles on these roads. After what seemed to be forever, we arrived at , where had already arrived on his motorcycle and was waiting for us. He proudly showed us his room. Each house is a room here. They are all round huts about 7 feet in diameter. All the building materials are obtained locally. The walls are mud walls and the mud is obtained just by digging it from the ground. The roofing materials are long sticks and grass, all harvested from around them early in the dry season. The sticks are tied with rope in a conical shape on top of the mud wall and the grass is woven and tied to the wooden roof structure layer by layer until a cap is put on top. There is a door but no window or ceiling. is from State, is 47 years old and his oldest child is 24. He and his wife chose to live here as missionaries, discipling and supervising the church planters.

We sat in the truck and ate our lunch which we had brought from Hotel, and then we parked our truck and prepared to move on. We were heading for and is the end of the road even for the truck. The church was planted and the church planted the church. Five motor cycles were brought, ’s and four others, and each of us got on the back of one. and had never been on a motorcycle before, but they were ready. It was a thirty minute motorcycle ride from to , and we had to pass through two dried up rivers. We passed a newly planted church at on our way. used to be all Moslems and planting a church there took persistence. went there 20 times before he was able to start the church. The motor cycle riders seemed to be experts at maneuvering on the winding roads and going in and out of gullies. In 30 minutes, we arrived in , where the members of the planted church were already gathered, waiting for us. Most of them had gone to the farm but we still had about 15 in the church. The church is rectangular, not round like the houses and there are no mud walls. It was upright sticks holding up the roof structure, and you have to bend down to enter. referred to the bending down as bowing before entering the church. The furniture was logs on the bare ground that you sat on. We encouraged them and prayed for them. The disciple who leads the church was said to be sick, so we decided to go and see him. When we saw he was sitting in an open round hut with no walls except for poles that hold the roof. This is like a sitting area. He looked very sick. His left hand was very swollen all the way to his wrist. explained that the problem is from the water. River blindness is a real problem in this whole area because the people use water from a pond used for everything. They drink from it, wash in it, use the water for cooking and the animals also walk into it and drink. said that many people in the community are blind. They have a great need for bore holes to give them good clean water. seemed to be in great pain but all we could do for him was pray. I wished we could help him take care of this infection that got his hand so swollen. And the real problem is the water supply.

explained that he was discipled by , he disciples and discipled who was taking care of the church at and discipling the people. We had four generations of disciples and lined them up for a photo. It is exciting to see generations of disciples and newly planted churches planting other churches all within one year.

We headed back on our motor cycles, and about half way back to , who had at the back of his motorcycle went down into the first dry river. I was on the motorcycle just behind them and the man taking me asked me to get down so he could push the motorcycle in and out at the other end. As I got down, I saw revving his motorcycle, trying to make it up the steep hill with sitting at the back. All of a sudden, the motorcycle, which had been smoking from stress, stopped going and started to roll back. I was scared and started to shout JESUS, JESUS. Before I knew it, the motorcycle had fallen on its side, fell backwards and on the side, partially under the motorcycle. I ran to and helped her to her feet. She staggered a little. But she was ok. She also kept saying I am a strong girl. I am ok. I grew up on the farm, I have taken falls before. I was relieved to see that she was indeed ok. She could have knocked her head hard on the ground or broken a bone but she only had some bruises and she later got back on the motorcycle, and we continued our journey.
